title: Slay the Spire
edition_pinned: PC stable build V2.3.4 (December 20, 2022). This pins the four-character game after the Watcher and balance updates, excluding Slay the Spire 2.
moat: Enemy intents price each card play while map branches price which future tests the deck must survive.

loop_skeleton.description: Choose a map node, read enemy intents, spend energy from a drawn hand, take or skip a reward, then route toward the next deck test.

depth_mechanisms.name: intent-priced turns
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Icons and numbers reveal each enemy's next action, so damage, block, setup, and potion use compete against a stated consequence.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If intent numbers are hidden, matched players take more preventable damage from underblocking than players shown the same encounters with intent numbers.

depth_mechanisms.name: uncorrelated card offers
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Each battle offers cards from a rarity-weighted pool without steering toward cards already taken, forcing adaptation instead of recipe completion.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If rewards always follow the deck's current tag, run-to-run drafts converge on a declared archetype and off-plan cards stop testing adaptation.

depth_mechanisms.name: route as resource forecast
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Branching paths let the player choose risk exposure against current health and deck needs before the next encounter.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If the map becomes a fixed corridor, players with different health and gold states traverse the same nodes on a matched seed instead of choosing different risk budgets.

depth_mechanisms.name: enemy portfolio tests
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Encounters pressure different deck properties, preventing one damage-block sequence from answering every fight.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If encounters test the same damage race, card picks converge on the highest output-per-energy line and utility cards lose draft value.

depth_mechanisms.name: cross-system combo assembly
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Cards, relics, upgrades, and potions alter draw, energy, scaling, and timing until a run-specific engine emerges.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If relics and upgrades only add damage, logged turns where they change draw, energy, or action order fall to zero.

feel_signature: The intent number sets pressure; cards snap into a sequence; damage or block resolves; the player then accepts, skips, or upgrades a future draw.
second_session_answer: The next run changes card offers, routes, relics, and encounter order, so the player tests whether prior matchup knowledge produces another deck rather than repeats one recipe.
packaging_lessons: Ship intent display with the first combat slice because hidden enemy action leaves the player without a basis for hand sequencing.
Package card rewards, route choice, and at least two encounter tests together; deckbuilding has no decision target when rewards do not answer future pressure.
Instrument card picks and win rates during content iteration, but retain designer review for game feel and difficulty.
Keep card text focused on the decision variables; move enemy forecasts into icons and numbers visible without inspection.
anti_lessons: Do not copy the act map, energy count, relic slots, and intent icons as a skin; preserve the decision function only after changing its mechanical inputs.
Do not steer reward generation toward a named archetype; the developer states that uncorrelated offers force adaptation.
Do not add card volume before encounter roles exist; without tests for the cards, choice count measures browsing rather than strategy.

system_bom.note: Node choice leads to intent-driven combat, reward choice, and another node.
Cards are the action vocabulary; draw order, energy cost, and upgrade state determine which sequences exist.
Relics and potions alter deck constraints or supply a one-use answer, linking encounter rewards to later turns.
Card additions, upgrades, and relics accumulate for the run and change later combat.
The generated map varies routes, enemies, card offers, relics, and bosses between climbs.
Defeat ends the run and returns the player to a generated climb with another strategy to assemble.
Intent icons and numbers plus card costs and text expose turn decisions; map paths expose route decisions.

citations.claim: Edition pin: V2.3.4 is the listed PC hotfix line for the original Slay the Spire, after the four-character release state.
citations.source: Mega Crit, “V2.3.4: Crash on launch fix for Non-English PC users,” Steam announcement, December 20, 2022: https://steamcommunity.com/ogg/646570/announcements/detail/5201125680698695379 ; Mega Crit press kit: https://www.megacrit.com/press-kits/slay-the-spire/

citations.claim: Moat: visible enemy intents make the hand a planning problem, while map paths add risk, reward, and planning across encounters.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d ; Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: Depth — intent-priced turns: Mega Crit added icons and numbers after hidden or click-to-inspect actions failed to support player and viewer planning.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019, intent-system discussion: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d

citations.claim: Depth — uncorrelated card offers: battle rewards vary each run and do not react to cards already acquired; rarity alone weights appearance.
citations.source: Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/ ; Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d

citations.claim: Depth — route as resource forecast: the developer identifies map navigation as a risk/reward and planning layer inspired by FTL.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020, path-choice discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i- ; Mega Crit, Slay the Spire Steam page, “An Ever-changing Spire”: https://store.steampowered.com/app/646570/Slay_the_Spire/

citations.claim: Depth — enemy portfolio tests: Mega Crit designed enemies to challenge different strategies and prevent one dominant strategy.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020, enemy-design discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: Depth — cross-system combo assembly: the design process rewards card synergies and run-specific combo engines; relics and potions add run inputs.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020, combo discussion: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i- ; Mega Crit press kit: https://www.megacrit.com/press-kits/slay-the-spire/ ; Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/

citations.claim: Feel signature: visible intent sets a numeric consequence, card order resolves the response, and post-battle card choice changes future draws.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019, intent-system discussion: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d ; Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/

citations.claim: Second-session answer: generated levels, varying card offers, deck permutations, and strategy-specific enemies force another adaptation rather than a fixed build order.
citations.source: Mega Crit press kit: https://www.megacrit.com/press-kits/slay-the-spire/ ; Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/ ; Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — intent first: the prototype without visible intent left players without a strategy basis; icons and numbers fixed the information gap.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019, intent-system discussion: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — rewards, routes, and encounter tests: path choice adds planning, while enemies test multiple strategies and rewards begin the next adaptation.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — instrumentation: Mega Crit used metrics and community feedback throughout early access while maintaining feel and difficulty.
citations.source: Anthony Giovannetti, “Slay the Spire: Metrics Driven Design and Balance,” GDC 2019, GDC Vault session 1025731

citations.claim: Packaging lesson — information compression: the intent system moved forecasts to visible icons and numbers so players focus on the decision variables.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019, intent-system discussion: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d

citations.claim: BOM — play-loop and skills: generated node selection frames turn-based card combat, then each battle presents a card decision that changes later hands.
citations.source: Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/

citations.claim: BOM — loot and progression: card additions and upgrades change later draws, relics change deck interactions, and potions provide encounter resources.
citations.source: Mega Crit, Slay the Spire Steam page: https://store.steampowered.com/app/646570/Slay_the_Spire/ ; Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/

citations.claim: BOM — missions: map navigation supplies a risk/reward planning layer, and generated levels vary the route between combat encounters.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i- ; Mega Crit, Slay the Spire Steam page, “An Ever-changing Spire”: https://store.steampowered.com/app/646570/Slay_the_Spire/

citations.claim: BOM — win-loss-death: failed fights restart the climb, and a new run begins carving another strategy from generated inputs.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: BOM — UI: intent icons and numbers expose enemy action without inspection; card and deck information supports draw-consistency decisions.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d ; Anthony Giovannetti, PlayStation Blog, May 13, 2019: https://blog.playstation.com/2019/05/13/how-to-come-out-on-top-in-slay-the-spire-out-may-21-on-ps4/

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — surface-copy inference: the cited design accounts tie map, energy, relic, and intent surfaces to information and adaptation functions, so copying surfaces alone does not preserve those functions.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d ; Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — reward steering: the developer states that card offers do not react to cards already acquired and require adaptation.
citations.source: Nintendo interview with Anthony Giovannetti, August 8, 2019: https://www.nintendo.com/jp/topics/article/1f6d2f1e-b7ea-11e9-b641-063b7ac45a6d

citations.claim: Anti-lesson — card-volume inference: Mega Crit designed enemies to test different strategies, establishing encounter roles as the decision target for card variety.
citations.source: Joel Couture interview with Anthony Giovannetti, Game Developer, January 22, 2020: https://www.gamedeveloper.com/game-platforms/road-to-the-igf-mega-crit-games-i-slay-the-spire-i-
